The MACC

observations.recommendation:
Adjust the GF books with the guidance of the following compound entry:
Due from Officers and Employees		     370,799.86
Other Payables					14,084,309.45
CIP						  3,759,263.32
Accounts Payable				           10,695,845.99;
Record all transactions due to contractors and suppliers to Accounts Payable account. Moreover, unpaid due to contractors must be recognized as Accounts Payable based on the progress billings as shown in the SWA and not based on obligation;
Monitor properly the balance of accounts to avoid incurring negative balance and adjust accordingly if the same occurred to reflect its proper recognition in the books; and
Review the credits in the Other Payables account under the TF and reclassify the same under their appropriate accounts using the following entry as a guide:
Other Payables					xxx
Due to National Government Agencies				xxx
Due to LGUs						xxx

We recommended that the MAcco reclassify the errors to their appropriate PPE accounts, and to transfer the cost of the constructed structures for use of the Barangays thru a Deed of Donation and derecognize the recorded values of the constructed structures in the book of the MLGU consistent with Section 267, Chapter 7 of GAM-LGUs.

We recommended that the MAcco prepare adjusting journal entries to correct the misclassification of accounts in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.
We recommended that the MAcco make the necessary adjusting entries to present the Due to LGUs and Other Payables accounts accurately as at year-end.

We recommended that the MAcco prepare the adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balance of the Due to NGAs account and other aforesaid affected accounts and to observe the proper classification of accounts in accordance with Section 4 (7) of PD No. 1445 and the RCA for LGUs.

We recommended that the MAcco make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balance of the affected accounts, Flood Control System, Accumulated Depreciation - Flood Control Systems and Depreciation - Infrastructure Assets, and henceforth, strictly observe the policies on the recognition of completed projects and depreciation of PPE.
Also, we recommended to the MAcco that completed projects be immediately reclassified to the appropriate asset accounts and the related depreciation be taken up in the books upon commencement of operation or usage.

We recommended that the MAcco prepare adjusting journal entries to correct the misclassification of accounts in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the MAcco coordinate with the identified agencies to reconcile and update their records and submit a copy of the reconciliation to the Audit Team and to ensure that the liquidations of the identified agencies be recorded in the proper accounting period to reflect the correct balance of the Due from LGUs.

We recommended that the MAcco, in addition to issuing demand letters, to further look into the status of the availability of the records or documents through the Accountable Officers responsible for handling the account as indicated in Item 7.1.f of COA Circular 2023-008 and be able to indicate the extent of validation made and prepare a detailed list of the lost documents. The result of which will be the initial step in the conduct of an investigation to be conducted by a duly created Investigation Committee.
We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation to Management that if all appropriate remedies to recover overdue receivables were already made, to comply with the set guidelines outlined in COA Circular No. 2023-008 on the one-time cleansing of dormant accounts, taking into consideration the timeframe prescribed thereto.

We recommended that the MAcco furnish documents to support the crediting of the prepayment or advance payment to Contractor 16 and make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect to correct balance of the Advances to Contractors account, and demand payment from Contractor 17 of the remaining unrecouped mobilization, otherwise, it will be disallowed in audit due to overpayment of contract.

We recommended that the MAcco maintain SLC and ensure compliance in the proper accounting of purchases of construction materials, and the OIC-GSO to ensure that SC are kept and up to date to reflect inventory receipts and issuances and to conduct periodic physical inventory counts to be reported in the RPCI and submitted to the Audit Team within the prescribed deadlines of July 31 and January 31 of each year.

We recommended Management to re-evaluate the deferred donation of the fire truck to BFP considering that the latter has direct control and actual usage of the property, and if in the affirmative, the MAcco to make the necessary adjusting entries duly supported by Deed of Donation.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended the following:
The Municipal Agriculturist to:
Submit the full inventory of breeding stocks and other biological assets of the MLGU if any;
Include in the inventory report of biological assets the prevailing market price for each item as of reporting date and information on the prevailing cost to sell of the biological assets; and
Furnish the report to the MAcco for recognition of any changes in the fair value less cost to sell of the biological assets; and
The MAcco to record the changes in fair value less cost to sell of the biological assets in the books of accounts.

Disclose the amount and details of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF in the Notes to the FS pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002; and
Record the asset or expense as acquired or incurred in the TF books to fairly present the balances of the assets, expenses and trust liabilities in the interim FS.

We recommended that the MAcco make the necessary restatement of the affected accounts in the FS and recognize the prior period errors at the earliest period presented in the comparative FS and properly identify prior period errors and provide adequate disclosure to properly account for the same.

We recommended that the MAcco strictly follow the prescribed accrual method in the recording of transactions and immediate recognition of the provision for depreciation in the books of account to ensure appropriate recognition in the FS of the periods to which it relates and to strictly adhere to the policies outlined in IPSAS 1 and 17.

We recommended that the MAcco ensure its disclosure fully comply with the provisions of Paragraph 129 of IPSAS 1 and Paragraph 36 of IPSAS 19, and explicitly identify whether any contingent liabilities exist in the Notes to FS.

We recommended that the MAcco observe the disclosure requirements in the preparation of FS in accordance with IPSASs 1 and 20 to ensure that the entity is transparent about its dealings with related parties and provide users with additional information in understanding the FS and aid in the decision-making as well as achieve fair presentation of the FS.

We recommended the following:
Management to clarify with the BIR the rule on taxability of cooperatives with respect to their transactions to non-members specifically on withholding of both income tax and VAT; and
The MAcco to always require the attachment of COTE on all payments made to cooperatives and after the clarification with BIR to withhold taxes on income payment to cooperatives if applicable. Further, require the cooperatives to furnish copies of their CDA registration and COTE, and the MT to remit on time all withheld taxes to the BIR.

We recommended that the MAcco resolve the above-mentioned reconciling items and identify the cause of discrepancies as well as locate all available data and supporting documents of the unreconciled items in the SL to come up with a more accurate and reliable information.
Likewise, we reiterated our previous recommendation that the MAcco immediately review the subsidiary schedules of the affected accounts with abnormal balances, analyze the recording of transactions which may have caused it, and effect the necessary adjustments to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.
Further, if once reconciled and resulted to unremitted premiums, we recommended that the MT strictly observe the prescribed due dates for the remittances of premium contributions and loans in compliance to the aforementioned GSIS and Pag-IBIG laws, rules and regulations in order to avoid incurrence of possible penalties.

We recommended that the ME and MAcco reconcile the Schedule of the CIP account with the Status of Projects Regardless of Source Report and make the necessary adjustments, then submit a copy of the reconciled reports to the Audit Team.
Once reconciled, we recommended that the MAcco make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balance of the affected accounts, CIP, Infrastructure Assets, Buildings and Other Structures and Depreciation accounts, if any.

We recommended the following:
The OIC-HRMO to (i) prepare a contract that states specific qualifications, tasks, duties, responsibilities, work schedule and time, and other roles and functions related to their job, and ensure the contract is perfected between the MLGU and the service provider, where both parties will agree between the terms and conditions by affixing their signatures; (ii) submit explanation in the hiring of three JOs with services rendered for private purposes and overstaffing of guards in the public market with overlapping schedules; and (iii) implement work schedules with defined areas of responsibility; and
The MAcco to ensure the completeness of supporting documents such as accomplishment reports to substantiate the actual services rendered by JOs.
Also, we recommended Management to continue its initial steps in adopting biometric system to digitalize timekeeping records and furnish the Audit Team progress report relative to its implementation.

We recommended the following:
The Gender Focal Point Systems to utilize HGDG tool as an assessment mechanism for a more substantiated accomplishment report and that the information contained therein is justified by evidence relevant for decision-making and budgeting. If trainings are necessary on the use of the tool, it may include said training in its GPB and may seek the assistance of an expert on gender analysis using the HGDG; and
Management as a whole to integrate in the budget possible response to the weaknesses identified by the HGDG tool to comply with the requirement of the MLGU to be gender sensitive and gender responsive.
We recommended Management to ensure compliance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations relative to premium contributions in order to avoid suspensions or disallowances in the future.

We recommended that the Management demand the contractors to issue Retention Money Bond (Surety Bond) or other prescribed forms for all completed projects which are still covered by the DLP.
If there are progress billings where 10% retention money is required, we recommended the following to Management to be used as guide in the assessment of releasing the retention money:
The ME to issue a Certification that the project is on schedule and has been satisfactorily completed in accordance with plans and specifications supported with Inspection Report; and
The MAcco to always ensure that the earlier said Certification with Inspection Report and other relevant documents were submitted together with the DVs of retention money plus the prescribed substitute pursuant to Item 6.2 Annex “E” of the RIRR of RA No. 9184 (Section 71.2.8 of the IRR of RA No. 12009).

We recommended that the BAC ensure submission of printout copies of Invitation to Bid posted in the PhilGEPS reflecting each appropriate status in reference to the PhilGEPS website bid notice status. Also, the Head of the BAC Secretariat to certify in writing that the Invitation to Bid has been posted in a conspicuous place and include the Certification in the procurement records submitted to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended that the BAC ensure the strict use of the RFQ form and other procurement forms prescribed by the GPPB to enhance the transparency, accountability, and the legitimacy of the procurement process.
A sample RFQ form can be found at https://www.gppb.gov.ph/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/RFQ-No.-019-2024.pdf

In addition, with respect to the parcels of land still in the name of private persons, we recommended Management to:
Initiate the transfer of OCT No. P-3498, OCT No. P-3781, OCT No. P-5379, OCT No. P-4611, OCT No. P-8017 and OCT No. P-7918 from the names of private persons to the name of the LGU; and
Tract an update about loan granted by the Rural Bank to the previous owner, and if already settled, initiate the removal of the annotation or Memorandum of Encumbrances on the said title.
We recommended the MAcco to reclassify the errors to their appropriate PPE accounts as guided by the following:

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting journal entries to correct the misclassification of account in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant resolve the above-mentioned reconciling items and identify the cause of discrepancies as well as locate all available data and supporting documents of the unreconciled items in the Subsidiary Ledgers to come up with a more accurate and reliable information.
Likewise, we recommended that Management through the Municipal Accountant immediately review the subsidiary schedules of the affected accounts with abnormal balances, analyze the recording of transactions which may have caused it, and effect the necessary adjustments to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.
Similarly, we recommended that Management identify the cause(s) of the deficiency and coordinate immediately with the GSIS to reconcile and update agency/employee records.
Further, if once reconciled and resulted to unremitted premiums, we recommended the MT to observe the prescribed due dates for the remittances of withheld taxes, premium contributions and loans in compliance to the aforementioned BIR, Pag-IBIG, PhilHealth and GSIS laws, rules and regulations in order to avoid incurrence of possible penalties.

We recommended Management to furnish the Office of the Auditor written explanation on the irregularities noted on the disbursements on Educational Assistance for evaluation in audit, otherwise, refund of the amounts disbursed shall be effected.

We recommended that Management stop utilizing the funds of the Municipality for private/religious purposes, and is enjoined to strictly abide by the existing laws, rules and regulations in order to protect the scarce resources of the government from improper use.

We recommended that the LCE, in collaboration with the Municipal SWD Officer and the SB, establish tailored guidelines and procedures for granting and disbursing donations. This should be done with thorough consideration of the social and living conditions of recipients to ensure a fair, just, and equitable distribution of funds.

We recommended that Management:
Immediately utilize the funds received from the NGAs in order for its constituents to timely receive the intended benefits of said PPAs;
Submit copies of the MOA entered into between the LGU and the DSWD, DOH, and BFP and other pertinent documents to facilitate the evaluation of the PPAs and their implementation; and
Enjoin the concerned Heads of Offices as the project implementors to submit written explanation regarding the delayed implementation of the projects funded by the NGAs in year 2023.

We recommended that Management cause the refund of the amount of ₱125,000.00 that was irregularly disbursed to three-termer public officials, otherwise, Notice of Disallowance shall be issued consistent with COA Circular No. 2012-003. In addition, Management is enjoined to strictly abide by the existing laws, rules and regulations in order to protect the scarce resources of the government from improper use.

We recommended that Management, through the LCE, enforce the immediate refund of the excessive Representation Allowance and Transportation Allowance (RATA) paid to the Municipal Vice Mayor and the SB Members, otherwise a Notice of Disallowance shall be issued pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-003.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant maintain complete subsidiary ledger for the 20% DF in the GF. Likewise, prepare the requisite financial reports for said special account, incorporating them into the schedules of the GF trial balance in accordance with the aforesaid guidelines as these will inform Management on the condition and status of operations of the special accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant comply with the set guidelines outlined in COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023 on the one-time cleansing of dormant accounts taking into consideration the timeframe prescribed thereto.

We recommended that Management enjoin the concerned Contractors to refund the corresponding overpayments made to them in view of the Permits, Clearances & Other Government Taxes treated separately from the Indirect Costs. Likewise, we recommended that Management enforce its computed liquidated damages against the defaulting Contractor and submit proof of refund together with the Statement of Work Accomplished as of the expiry date (January 18, 2023) for validation.
Further, we recommended the procuring entity’s representative or project engineer to thoroughly review the contractor’s Statement of Work Accomplished before certifying the amount to be paid to ensure it aligns with the contract costs, thus safeguarding government funds against any potential overpayments.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries relative to the misclassified/misstated accounts;
Require the Accounting Unit and Property and Supply Unit to maintain Stock Cards for all relief goods procured for DRMM and reconcile their reports to establish the correct balances; and
Strictly and prudently comply with all the rules and regulations laid out relative to LDRRMF.

We recommended to the LCE the following:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ascertain that future disbursements of RATA are substantiated with all the necessary documentations; and
Enjoin the municipal officials and heads of offices to promptly furnish the Office of the Auditor with a duly signed Certification affirming that the Official/Employee did not use a government vehicle in CY 2023, or if otherwise, furnish a Certification on the use of government vehicle with the specific day/s of actual use. Additionally, require them to submit a complete set of Daily Time Records (DTRs), approved leave applications, and approved Travel Orders to substantiate the RATA disbursed in year 2023. If not complied, it may result in the issuance of a Notice of Suspension.
Further, we recommended that Management require the municipal officials and heads of offices to specify the (1) type of leave applied for in a particular day on their DTRs and (2) mode of transportation on their Travel Orders to facilitate the pre-audit by the Municipal Accountant on the propriety and validity of their future RATA claims. Likewise, we recommended the submission of written explanation on why RATA was paid in advance as it is contrary to Section 88 (1) of PD No. 1445 and cease from doing so in the succeeding RATA payments.

We recommended Management to:
Instigate competitive bidding or explore suitable alternative mode of procurement in contracting the services of Consultants clothed as EAs and submit to the Office of the Auditor pertinent reports and documents in relation to this initiative;
Submit copy of the Authorization by the SB to the LCE to enter into Contract to the EAs/Consultants in behalf of the LGU; and
Stop contracting the services of Consultants (1) with redundant functions with the regular LGU employees and (2) who perform services that redound to the benefit of the LMP, not the LGU, since these are unnecessary and irregular expenditures, respectively, which shall be disallowed in audit pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-003.
Likewise, we recommended Management to be always judicious and prudent in the use of government funds and shall continue to ensure that no irregular, unnecessary, extravagant, excessive and unconscionable expenses are incurred by the government in line with Administrative Order No. 6 dated September 19, 2017.

We recommended the following:
The Municipal DRRM Officer to provide a written justification as to the improper utilization of the 30% QRF considering the proximity of the dates of the onslaught of the typhoon, the provincial declaration and release of the financial assistance;
Management to explain in writing the excessive release of financial assistance when compared to the total loss reported on the Final Municipal Damage Assessment Report; and
Management, through the Municipal DRRM Officer, to observe the provisions of Section 21 of RA No. 10121 or Philippine DRRM Act of 2010.

We recommended that Management provide its written justification on the legality and reasonableness of the expenditures. In addition, we recommended that Management stop granting financial assistance intended for private purposes and if not, it shall result in these expenditures being subject to audit disallowances.

We recommended that Management consider the payment of JOs assigned in projects under the 20% DF be charged against the MOOE to make way for other projects in the AIP under the said fund be optimally funded. Further, we recommended the Municipal Budget to ensure that disbursements of wages do not exceed the limit set forth by the laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management strictly observe the prescribed period of payment of UCA which is the month of April as explicitly stated in the aforesaid DBM issuances in order to avoid future audit disallowances.

We recommended that Management provide a written justification as to the improper utilization of the 70% Mitigation Fund and strictly and prudently comply with all the rules and regulations laid out relative to LDRRMF.

We recommended Management to diligently execute payments directly to the creditors, in accordance with Section 93 of PD No. 1445. This approach aims to prevent any potential misuse or misappropriation by the recipients of the assistance allocated for beneficiary-schools. Furthermore, for subsequent payments of a similar nature, we recommended Management to demand ORs from the recipient schools to ensure and establish accountability.

We recommended Management to strictly abide by the reportorial requirements, procedures and guidelines set forth in COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015 to fully strengthen peace and order in the community that will benefit its constituents.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor, being the incumbent President of LMP Nueva Vizcaya Chapter, to requisition official receipt Accountable Form No. 51 for the official use of the League of Municipalities of Nueva Vizcaya to acknowledge all receipts due to the League, to fully establish financial accountability and to protect public funds to proper use.

We recommended that the LCE direct the AO to liquidate her CAs within the prescribed period and ensure that no additional CA shall be allowed unless the previous ones are settled, properly accounted and documented. Lastly, the DO and other concerned officials and employees should strictly comply with the laws, rules and regulations cited above on the grant and liquidation of CAs.

We recommended to Management that the appropriated amounts of the 20% DF be fully utilized for the implementation and accomplishments of the lined-up projects contained in the approved AIP in order to optimally attain its desired benefits to the advantage of its constituents.

We recommended that Management, as a withholding agent, ensure that taxes be withheld on payment of BAC honoraria and promptly remit to the BIR said taxes withheld within the prescribed period to avoid penalties, surcharges and interests which if in any case of late remittance, it shall be personally borne by the defaulting LGU officials concerned. Likewise, proper accounting of the transaction must be observed by the Municipal Accountant to present fairly the FSs.

We recommended that the Gender Focal Point System Officer ensure the annual preparation of the GAD Plan using the format in Annex A of this report which will be the basis of the GAD budget in the Local Expenditure Program and submit the same to the office of the auditor. Further, the GAD Plan should include the element of Institutionalizing GAD Database/Sex-disaggregated Data which could show a holistic picture of the organization with respect to implementation of a gender- responsive institution. This could be done through survey as data-gathering tools and summarized for basis of GAD plan preparation.

We recommended that the Gender Focal Point System Officer ensure compliance with the required format of the GAD AR as presented in Annex B of PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2012-01 and prepare the necessary attachments for complete support of such accomplishments.

We recommended that Management adhere to the funding requirement of one percent of its IRA to support its Local Councils for Protection of Children, as mandated by Section 15 of RA No. 9344. Upon allocation, it is essential to guarantee the effective implementation of priority plans for children, such as those outlined in Item II of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-150.

The LO must immediately issue an OR amounting to ₱1,616.18 as acknowledgment of the cash overage and/or the submission of necessary documents or written explanation why overage occurred;
The LO must diligently must verify all reports of collections turned over to her by the Collectors to ascertain the accuracy of the collections remitted and to properly establish accountability in her hand; and
The LO and MAcco should adjust the balance of accountability per cashbook and general ledger, respectively, to reflect the reconciled balance of accountability as of September 18, 2023 per cash examination.

We recommended that management judiciously plan and schedule its PPAs for each budget year to expedite implementation of all prioritized PPAs to optimize the utilization of the 20% development fund as mandated under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1.

We recommended that management through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office:
Monitor the utilization of LDRRMF focusing on programs, projects and activities (PPAs) requiring the highest priority in terms of urgency and/or impact on the four thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management; and
Ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects identified during each budget year to optimize the utilization of the fund and to warrant that goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities are achieved.

We recommended that Management:
Prioritize construction of structures as contained in the SWMP in order to obtain optimum result in the operation of its SLF; and
Facilitate the full implementation of long overdue Solid Waste Management Plan - PPAs funded under the 20% development fund.
We recommended that Management observe the abovementioned provisions of Local Budget Circular No. 98 on Personnel Services (PS) limitation on Local Government Budgets to avoid audit disallowances.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 2016 - 130 – to regularly withhold and remit to the BIR the three percent (3%) percentage tax due from every payment of wages made to Job Orders and Contract of Service to avoid commencement of appropriate legal action from the Bureau.

We recommended that Management prudently evaluate all future grants and donations to be extended to the constituents of the Municipality whether these actually supports public welfare in consonance with Section 4(2) of PD 1445 and Section 335 of RA 7160.

We recommended that Management institutionalize implementing guidelines and policies to regulate the processing and approval of grants and donations, an Executive Order to that effect is highly encouraged.

We recommended that the Municipality, through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office, prudently evaluate its compliance with the Minimum Standards on Disaster Preparedness of the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council and conform accordingly with its provisions to achieve desired goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the LGU.

We recommended that Management, whenever the need arises to grant cash advances to officers other than those existing Disbursing Officers, ensure that authority issued by the Head of the Agency is availed of and documented, along with the certification from the Accountant that previous cash advances have been liquidated and accounted for in the books, and the duly approved application for fidelity bond adequately covering total cash advances granted.

We recommended that Management require full liquidation of all outstanding cash advances and refrain from granting additional cash advances unless previous cash advances are fully liquidated in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Management support all requests for procurement of drugs and medicines with a certification signed by the requisitioning officer that the drug products being requisitioned or procured fall within and conform with PNDF Volume I, current edition.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (Disbursing Officer) do the following:

Reconcile its records with the accounting office to identify necessary adjustments, if any. The result of the reconciliation should be duly documented and a copy of which furnished to the Office of the Auditor for evaluation; and
Produce immediately the missing funds and/or present necessary documents evidencing prior deposits/remittances/liquidations not yet taken up in the books as stated above.

We recommended that the Disbursing Officer secure all necessary documents to corroborate the transactions as presented under Annex I and J of this report for review by the Audit Team. Moreover, on all future disbursements, before the actual release of fund, ensure that all necessary documents were already secured to corroborate the liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended the following:
At the close of each business day, collectors shall turn over all their collections intact to the Liquidating Officer and prepare the corresponding Report of Collections and Deposits;
Strictly monitor accountable forms issued, including those of the barangays, and remind concerned accountable officers of their responsibility to remit their collections as prescribed above; and
Require accountable officers to report and remit first all accountable forms received and their collections prior to issuance of additional accountable forms.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer monitor the actual turnover of the inventory of accountable forms to establish specific reorder points and economic order quantity so as not to over purchase supplies and to prevent voluminous quantities of unused accountable forms throughout the year.

We recommended that Accountable Officers maintaining cashbooks regularly accomplish the certification of transactions at the end of each month as required under Section 63 (E) of the Local Treasury Operations Manual.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer maintain and regularly update a cashbook as prescribed under Section 63(A)(3) of the Local Treasury Operations Manual (LTOM), and ensure that these are sufficiently safeguarded.

We recommended that the Accounting Office maintain in sufficient detail its records to conveniently establish specific balances of accountabilities per account, and thereafter, the Municipal Treasurer reconcile her cash book records with the accounting ledgers every end of the month or as the need arises. Moreover, discrepancies identified should be adjusted accordingly to come up with updated, accurate and reliable reports.

We recommended that management ensure that persons making deposits/withdrawals are escorted by armed guards or any capable personnel, and that transportation equipment is provided to ensure the security and safety of the Accountable officers as well as the funds under their custody.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly comply with the provisions of Section 88 of the General Provisions of Republic Act No. 10924, otherwise known as the General Appropriations Act of 2017, on the submission of AAPSI report to facilitate timely validation on the actions taken by the LGU on COA findings and recommendations.

The Audit Team recommended that Management undertake a complete periodic physical inventory of municipal properties and immediately reconcile the balances of any affected accounts to permit fair presentation of PPE in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
The Audit Team recommended management that:

All accountable officers must consume one (1) booklet at a time and refrain from simultaneously issuing AFs from two or more booklets. In addition, AOs must ensure that all pertinent information and data in the AFs are properly accomplished.

Adequate storage facilities be provided to safeguard documents from loss, theft or deterioration and to effectively monitor the flow of AFs.

Prior to issuance and use of accountable forms, accountable officers must carefully examine each booklet or pad and immediately report any defects found therein.

All AOs be directed to conscientiously abide with all relevant procedures in the requisition and issuance of Accountable Forms. Further, all AOs must immediately cease from the practice of borrowing or transferring AFs.

All accountable officers be directed to consume first all accountable forms still in their possession before requesting for additional quantities. In particular, the Municipal Treasurer should implement adequate control procedure in the requisition and issuance of Accountable Forms.

A personnel whose task is to ensure prompt and proper preparation of the Consolidated Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms be appointed.

At the close of each business day, Collectors/Tellers shall turn over all their collections intact to the Local Treasurer/Cashier and prepare the corresponding Report of Collections and Deposits. An Office Order may be issued to remind accountable officers of their responsibility to remit their collections on time or as soon as their collections reach ₱5,000.00.

The Office of the Municipal Treasurer maintain a record book of each collector for proper monitoring of their remittances.

Direct each accountable officer to maintain an updated cashbook of his own which is separate and distinct from that of the others.

All transactions involving collections as well as disbursements are entered in the appropriate cashbook at the end of each day. The accountable officers should be reminded that they are entirely accountable for each cashbook under their custody and should the responsibility in the preparation of the same be delegated, essential controls must be placed to ensure accurate entries thereon.

Both the Accounting and Treasury Offices to reconcile their accounts every month or as often as it is needed.

The Treasury Office be provided with adequate vaults and other storage facilities to ensure the safety and integrity of records and other relevant reports.
